name: Manavala Mamunigal

dates_evidence.description: Tenkalai sectarian chronicles document his life-dates.
Manuscript colophons across his commentary corpus.

core_thesis: Maṇavāḷa Māmunigaḷ writes the exhaustive Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa *vyākhyāna*-corpus on Pillai-Lokācārya's *aṣṭādaśa-rahasya* and on Nāmmāḻvār's *Tiruvāymoḻi* through which the Tenkalai school becomes an enduring tradition (the standard English-language secondary engagement with this *vyākhyāna*-tradition is Clooney's *Seeing Through Texts*, 1996; the work is not on disk in our corpus, cited via title-thesis and known scholarly summary). The architecture is preserved without revision — Brahman as Nārāyaṇa-with-Śrī qualified by *cit* and *acit* in *apṛthak-siddha* relation (*Mumukṣuppadi-Vyākhyāna* on Pillai-Lokācārya §§1–25, on *tirumantra*); *prapatti* as the sole *upāya* (*Mumukṣuppadi-Vyākhyāna* on §§116–166, on *dvaya-mantra*); Bhagavān's *kṛpā* as sole effective cause of *mokṣa* (*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa-Vyākhyāna* on Pillai-Lokācārya §§270–306, the *upāya-prakaraṇa*); Śrī as *upāya* alongside Nārāyaṇa (*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa-Vyākhyāna* on §§9–42, the *puruṣakāra-prakaraṇa*); *bhāgavata-śeṣatva* as the highest status (*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa-Vyākhyāna* on §§406–438, the *puruṣakāra-vaibhava-prakaraṇa*). Two consolidating emphases are his own. First, the integration of *prabandham*-veneration with rahasya-doctrine: the Āḻvārs' Tamil hymns, *Tiruvāymoḻi* especially, are not merely devotional but doctrinally authoritative as *Drāviḍa-Veda*, *śruti*-equivalent in interpretive force (*Tiruvāymoḻi-Vyākhyāna* on the *avatārikā* and on *Tiruvāymoḻi* 1.1.1; cf. his *Upadeśa-Ratnamālai* vv. 39–42 on the *prabandham* as Veda-equivalent). The Vaḍakalai treats the Tamil hymns as devotionally authoritative but doctrinally subordinate to the Sanskrit Upaniṣads; Māmunigaḷ's commentary on *Tiruvāymoḻi* enacts the contrary settlement. Second, the doctrine of *ācārya-abhimāna* — the *ācārya*'s love as the operative mediation of grace (*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa-Vyākhyāna* on Pillai-Lokācārya §§439–450, the *ācārya-niṣṭhā-prakaraṇa*). The surrendered self's salvation is not enacted by the *jīva*'s effort but by being received into the *ācārya-paramparā* whose origin is Nārāyaṇa himself; the *ākiñcana*'s incapacity (which on Pillai-Lokācārya is what makes *prapatti* possible at all, *Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* §83 with Māmunigaḷ's gloss) is here radicalised, since even the recognition of incapacity is mediated through the *ācārya*. He is traditionally invoked as the second *ācārya* after Rāmānuja (the verse *śrī-śailesa-dayā-pātraṃ …* extends *rāmānujāya namaḥ* into the present).

engaged_works.title: Mumukshuppadi-Vyakhyana
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on Pillai-Lokācārya's Mumukṣuppadi.
engaged_works.summary: Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa commentary on Pillai-Lokācārya's *Mumukṣuppadi*, the canonical Tenkalai exposition of the three esoteric *mantra*-s — *tirumantra* (*Mumukṣuppadi* §§1–115), *dvaya-mantra* (§§116–217), *carama-śloka* (§§218–278) — and the school's standard catechetical handbook. Māmunigaḷ glosses each of the 278 *cūrṇikā*-s with three layers added to the original: cross-references to the entire Āḻvār *prabandham*-corpus, citations from Nāthamuni, Yāmuna, and Rāmānuja's Sanskrit works, and the lineage's oral expositions transmitted through the post-Pillai-Lokācārya generations. The commentary fixes the Tenkalai settlement at every contested point. The conditionless construal of *prapatti* is grounded in the *carama-śloka*'s *parityajya* (vyākhyāna on §§246–271), taken to require renunciation of the *aṅga*-conditions themselves as instruments. The rejection of *bhakti-yoga* as a separate *upāya* for the *ākiñcana* is defended by the argument that *bhakti-yoga* presupposes the very competence the *ākiñcana* lacks (vyākhyāna on §§271–278). Śrī's joint *upāya*-status with Nārāyaṇa is fixed against the Vaḍakalai *puruṣakāra*-only reading (vyākhyāna on §§116–166, on the *dvaya*'s first half). *Ācārya-abhimāna* is articulated as the operative mediation of grace. The work matters because it is through this commentary, not through the *Mumukṣuppadi* alone, that Tenkalai students learn the rahasya-tradition; the commentary is treated as inseparable from the original text.

engaged_works.title: Yatindra-Pravana-Prabhava
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored hagiographic-doctrinal text on Rāmānuja.
engaged_works.summary: A Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa work on Rāmānuja (*Yatīndra* — "king of ascetics") and the lineage of *ācārya*-s flowing from him, fusing hagiography with doctrinal exposition. The lives of the *ācārya*-s are themselves the argument for the school's soteriology: they exemplify the operation of *ācārya-abhimāna* and *kṛpā* in concrete generations, and the doctrinal claim that grace is mediated through the lineage is established by the historical existence of the lineage itself. Māmunigaḷ traces the descent from Nāthamuni through Yāmuna to Rāmānuja and then through the post-Rāmānuja Tenkalai succession — Kūrattāḻvān, Mudaliyāṇḍān, Embār, Bhaṭṭar, Nañjīyar, Periyavāccān Piḷḷai, Vaḍakku-tiru-vīdhi-piḷḷai, Pillai-Lokācārya — establishing that *prapatti* is enacted in the *ācārya-paramparā* and that one's salvation is a function of being received into it. The work institutionalises the Tenkalai self-conception as the authentic post-Rāmānuja lineage and provides the textual basis for the *paramparā*-veneration that orders Tenkalai community-life. It is also a major source for the school's reception-history of the *Śrīvaiṣṇava-Guru-Paramparā* tradition.

engaged_works.title: Commentaries on Pillai Lokacharya's rahasya corpus
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentaries on the aṣṭādaśa-rahasya.
engaged_works.summary: Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa commentaries on Pillai-Lokācārya's *aṣṭādaśa-rahasya* — the eighteen secret tracts, including *Tattva-Trayam*, *Artha-Pañcaka*, *Prameya-Śekhara*, *Sāra-Saṃgraha*, *Yādṛcchika-Padi*, *Prapanna-Paritrāṇa*, culminating in the *Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa*. The commentary on *Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* is foundational for the school. It fixes the Tenkalai reading of the five most contested Pillai-Lokācārya theses against the Vaḍakalai of Vedānta Deśika: *prapatti* is conditionless (vyākhyāna on §§270–306); Bhagavān's *kṛpā* is the sole effective cause of *mokṣa* (vyākhyāna on §§270–306); Śrī is *upāya* alongside Nārāyaṇa (vyākhyāna on §§9–42); *bhāgavata-śeṣatva* exceeds direct *Bhāgavat-śeṣatva* (vyākhyāna on §§406–438); sin after surrender is itself an occasion for the Lord's forgiving love (vyākhyāna on §§356–393, the *aparādha-prakaraṇa*). The commentary on *Tattva-Trayam* gives the canonical Tenkalai exposition of the *cit*–*acit*–*Īśvara* ontology in Maṇipravāḷa (vyākhyāna on the three *prakaraṇa*-s respectively), paralleling — and on Tenkalai reading completing — Vaḍakalai's Sanskrit *Tattva-Muktā-Kalāpa* tradition. Together with the *Tiruvāymoḻi*-commentary, these constitute the largest single commentarial corpus in Tenkalai literature and the textual basis on which the school's distinct Maṇipravāḷa scholasticism rests.

engaged_works.title: Tiruvāymoḻi commentary
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa commentary on Nāmmāḻvār's Tiruvāymoḻi.
engaged_works.summary: A Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa commentary on Nāmmāḻvār's *Tiruvāymoḻi* — the 1,102-verse Tamil hymn-cycle treated as *Drāviḍa-Veda* and doctrinally authoritative in the Tenkalai school. The commentary stands in the line of the earlier Tenkalai *vyākhyāna*-s — Tirukkurukai-pirān-piḷḷān's *Āṛāyirappaḍi*, Naṅjīyar's *Onpadināyirappaḍi*, Periyavāccān-piḷḷai's *Irupattu-nālāyirappaḍi*, Vaḍakku-tiru-vīdhi-piḷḷai's *Muppattāṛāyirappaḍi* — citing them throughout and reading every *pāsuram* under the *rahasya*-doctrine of Pillai-Lokācārya. Each *pāsuram* is read at three registers simultaneously: devotional (the Āḻvār's longing, separation, return), doctrinal (Brahman *saviśeṣa*, *cit*-*acit*-*Īśvara*, *śeṣatva*), and soteriological (*prapatti* enacted, *kṛpā* received, *kaiṅkarya* anticipated). The Āḻvār's surrender to Bhagavān at Śrīraṅgam (vyākhyāna on *Tiruvāymoḻi* 5.8 and 6.10, *agalakillen* and *akalakillen*) is taken as the lived enactment of *prapatti* in its fullest form, and so as the operative paradigm of the school's soteriology. The doctrinal opening at vyākhyāna on *Tiruvāymoḻi* 1.1.1 (*uyarvaṟa uyar-nalam*) establishes the Tamil hymn's Veda-equivalence in interpretive force. The work cements the Tenkalai claim that Nāmmāḻvār's Tamil *prabandham* is *śruti*-equivalent and that experiential surrender is the school's primary norm, complementing — and on Tenkalai reading exceeding in directness — the Sanskrit Vedānta tradition.
